feedback.text: I understand and agree that being a non-EU citizen my feedback on this won't necessarily be considered, but I still urge it to be. The nature of the internet is so that every product distribution that happens online has the potential of reaching the whole world, where internet access exists and where the distributor of the product hasn't placed direct prohibition for distribution. A law that enables protection for Digital Distribution is a law that helps the whole world, even if only tangentially. These laws might not protect international purchases by non-citizens, but the simple existence of such laws inhibits bad practices by third parties or companies. This law would also setup a precedent for other countries to follow suit. Brazil implemented the LGPD to reaffirm the extension of its laws protecting personal data in 2018 and giving more solid ground for contesting data ownership and its removal, two years after GDPR issue at 2016. Even if the LGPD was already in consideration prior to GDPR and works through different means, the precedence existing helps legislators. Not only that, this law can be made to extend helping protect consumer rights on games, either produced or distributed on the European Union. The French company Ubisoft, has terminated several of its games that required minimal internet connection under the reasoning that they couldn't support the product anymore. One of the most recent games being shutdown was The Crew (2018-2024), not long after the release of the third game of the series, The Crew Motorfest (2023-current), a shutdown that affected citizens everywhere in the world where the game was distributed, not only EU citizens.
